title: How to reduce injury risk an injector. 5 simple steps you can do.
filename: How to reduce injury risk an injector. 5 simple steps you can do..en.vtt
excerpt: What is necrosis why you should never see it and what you need to know as an injector so although this is the worst thing that can happen to one of your patients I believe that there are five things that you can do that will reduce your risk and I'm going to be covering those tow…

segments.text: what is necrosis why you should never see it and what you need to know as an injector so although this is the worst thing that can happen to one of your patients I believe that there are five things that you can do that will reduce your risk and I'm going to be covering those towards the end of the show before we dive in if you're interested in joining a group of medical professionals and to master things such as anatomy and injection technique I'm starting a new movement there's a waiting list link down below click on that and sign up so let me give you the five strategic goals that you should have to never have a necrotic injury in your clinic firstly we have to accept that we cannot lower the risk of vascular injury to zero there's always some probability this means that we need a system that puts the patient in the best possible case scenario at every point along a whole patient Journey so the patient journey is a very important thing to understand it's never just about the injection technique or whether you did one safety step one is if you're going to have a vascular cusion make sure it's a small vascular cusion inject in such a way that even if you're in a vessel you

don't spend so long in that vessel that you cause a massive vascular cusion so this is down to for example using a canul but moving in all sorts of directions as's many directions and sliding in and out so you can lower the risk of severity of vascular cusion which is different to the frequency of vascular cusion by thinking about how much product you put in any particular area so you can do multiple small boluses on the periostium instead of one big one for example you can keep the needle moving which is a valid way of Distributing the risk but it doesn't decrease the frequency of vascular occlusion it actually increases it step two is to lower the frequency of vascular cusion now I separate these things out because people often get confused with severity and frequency and many of our safety steps are actually pulling an opposite direction for example the moving needle technique increases frequency but it decreases severity of vascular cusion with the Hope being that the severity is so small that it's almost insignificant if you block a vessel there's a lot to unpack about this and it may not be true the

way many people are doing it so how do we decrease the frequency if you are about to inject is there anything you can do that would detect a high-risk needle placement so for example if you aspirate it's not 100% protective but you will detect probably around 50% of the time that you're intravascular and you can also increase that further by having a negative pressure in the syringe and doing small movements in the area you're about to inject this is old medicine everyone who's ever taken blood has known that if you put your needle in to take blood and try and draw back and nothing comes out to move your needle because you scan a greater area for the position of your artery or Your Vessel if you're taking blood and it's a simple way of slightly nudging the risk in your favor in terms of detecting an intravascular placement it doesn't matter if you're in a tiny vessel that doesn't aspirate because those are not the ones that cause these large vascular clusions we want to detect intravascular placement in large named vessels you can also Al lower the frequency of vascular collusion by always injecting in a way

that makes it hard to culate vessels so for example if you're injecting at 90° to a vessel even if you do go through that vessel you'll spend far less time in it than if you're injecting parallel to the vessel and you enter the vessel this is another simple way of decreasing the frequency of vascular cusion depth of injection is another way to decrease frequency as long as you always inject in the plane where the vessels are least likely to be which is either the upper dermis or the periosteum as long as you're not know near foran you will always be injecting where vessels are less likely to be and this is another anatomical way of reducing risk so even if you do all of these steps and all the others that you can add to it there is still another issue we are probably going to get a vascular cusion one day anyway so part three is to make sure you diagnose quickly we need to have systems and processes that enable us to diagnose a blocked vessel as soon as possible one of the simplest things to do is to check capil refill immediately after every injection I think this is the reason why in my clinic where we've had 27 vascular occlusions on record that all of them

were diagnosed and treated immediately is because all of my clinicians were constantly being trained to check compl refill before you discharge the patient when you get into the swing of this it's effortless because you're always cleaning the skin anyway as you push on it you just observe blood flow but quick diagnosis is not quite enough so part four is about diagnosing comprehensively and here we have an issue I've seen many times around my network is that clinicians will get the diagnosis correct but they will underdiagnose how severe it is an example of this would be someone injects a small area of the lip they get a vascular cusion which they identify through poar in the lip and they initiate a reversal process send the patient home and then it turns out 24 hours later that the patient has vascular compromise affecting their chin and their lower neck now this was all down to underdiagnosis it's actually something you will see play out even in small vascular cusions that they initially seem smaller than they actually are at least the ones I've seen there's an initial diagnosis and over the course of a couple of hours you realize the area is a little bit bigger than what you

first thought so it's very important to spend time diagnosing comprehensively the most obvious example of this is when you actually have deeper tissue injury that you can't see so for example a chin injection that affects the tongue or a lip injection that affects the nose or a nose injection that affects the septum these are all examples where the injury is actually worse than you first think so it's really important to spend some time getting a comprehensive diagnosis part five is about reversing comprehensively so if you've used a non-reversal product which you would have done if you had thought hard about how to reduce severity then you will have the chance to reverse the process some clinicians undertreat at this point now this is just my opinion and it's very hard to prove scientifically but I believe it's better to overtreat than undertreat vascular cusions and I've seen a number of vascular cusions through our Network where they followed a relatively lowd dose protoc so for example 1500 unit vial discharge the patient see them back in 24 hours

and those patients still got necrotic injuries so I believe it's better to keep your patient with you until you have capillar refill restored so long as you have the type of product that you can do this with then release and you should see blood flow back into the lips and to keep going in all the areas that are affected with repeated high dose hyon days injections massage warm compress until blood flow is normal now I've heard of cases where this has gone on now for 8 to 12 hours with one vascular cusion if you've injected small amounts and done everything you can do to reduce the severity this is unlikely the vas conclusions that I've seen take around 90 minutes to 2 hours before blood flow starts to return um but there will be some exceptions so it depends a lot on how you've injected i' like to learn more about how this process actually unfolds with the real case make sure you click on Julie Bass kaplan's video which I did with her because that really captures some of the important details that you will never hear in the papers that are published about this subject
also don't forget to sign up to my waiting list if you are thinking about joining my movement if you want to learn more and progress safely it's the place to [Music] be
